evidence_entries.evidence_text: Miles. Yeah. This most unique member of the Dude Ranchers Association is laid out to look just like a town out of the Old West. But behind the doors of what appears to be the mining office, Miss Kitty's bordello, or Doc Holliday's dentist chair, are state-of-the-art rooms, decorated in an 1880s flair. We live in the newspaper. Juergen von Wolf and his wife Karen were here from Germany on a springtime visit to the ranch, their seventh in just five years, with plans for two more trips by the end of the year. it's the people it's the scenery it's surroundings it's the horses it's the personnel a lot of people ask

When you're walking around, you'll feel it. And it's just like walking the streets of Tombstone. That famous town is just two miles away. We'll take you for a visit in just a bit. But even closer stands the namesake of Tombstone Monument Ranch, the 25-foot tall stone monument to Tombstone founder Ed Sheflin. The Apaches ruled this country when the prospector came here looking for gold. Friends warned all he'd find in the desert would be his own tombstone. And he came close one day when he hid out from an Apache war party in some rocks where that monument now stands, which you can visit on a trail ride. But when Sheflin discovered the silver strike that made him rich, he had the last laugh, naming his new town

OCR on monument frames repeatedly detects the founder surname and a death line. Relevant readings include: frame 165.00s: "EC SCHIEFFELIN" and "DIED MAYIL,IS97"; frame 166.00s: "LCICHICEFELIN" and "D160 MN 12,1897"; frame 182.00s: "EC SCHIEFFELIN" and "DIED NN12,1897". Other frames contain noisier variants such as "DIEL MR12,1807" and "DIEL MAY12,1807".

inference_steps.text: The narrator describes the rooms/bedrooms as "decorated in an 1880s flair," so the bedroom-related year must be some year in the 1880s, though the transcript itself does not specify which exact year.
The monument/history segment identifies the founder being referenced as Ed Sheflin/Schieffelin.

The OCR from the monument frames repeatedly pairs the Schieffelin name with a death line ending in 1897, while alternative 1807 readings appear as noisier variants. Because multiple frames independently support 1897, the grounded founder death year is 1897.

Using the grounded death year 1897 and the answer choices, the needed bedroom year is the 1880s value that makes one listed sum: 3777 - 1897 = 1880, 3775 - 1897 = 1878, 3769 - 1897 = 1872, 3762 - 1897 = 1865, and 3773 - 1897 = 1876. Only 1880 falls in the 1880s, so the exact year selected from the decade phrase is 1880.
Therefore the sum is 1897 + 1880 = 3777, which matches option B.

findings.message: The trace turns the transcript phrase "decorated in an 1880s flair" into the exact year 1880 by reverse-engineering from the answer choices. The provided text explicitly says the transcript does not specify which exact year in the 1880s, so selecting 1880 is not grounded by evidence about what year the narrator used.
Because the bedroom year is not textually grounded as an exact year, the final choice B. 3777 is not justified from the evidence package even though the arithmetic from 1897 + 1880 is correct.

audit_report.feedback: The founder death year is reasonably supported as 1897, but the trace fails by converting the decade description "1880s flair" into the exact year 1880 using the options. The answer needs grounded justification for the exact bedroom year asked for, or must remain unresolved if the prompt/evidence does not legitimately supply one.
missing_information: A textually grounded exact year that the narrator used to describe the bedrooms, rather than only the decade phrase "1880s flair"
A justified mapping from the grounded bedroom year and founder death year to one unique answer option

captions.visual: A group of people in cowboy hats ride horses down a dusty, unpaved street in a classic Western town. The buildings are made of wood and stone, with a prominent covered porch. A lower-third graphic appears, identifying the location as 'TOMBSTONE MONUMENT RANCH, TOMBSTONE, ARIZONA'. The riders, including a woman in a green top, slow their horses to a stop near a wooden fence.

video_background: The setting is a Western-themed town with wooden storefronts, a covered porch, and a clear blue sky. The ground is a dusty, reddish-brown dirt road.
storyline: This segment introduces the setting of Tombstone, Arizona, establishing the Western theme of the location through the use of horseback riding and period-appropriate attire.
shooting_style: This is a standard establishing shot sequence, cutting between different angles to show the riders and their destination.

captions.visual: A woman in a green plaid top and a white cowboy hat dismounts her horse. She holds the reins and walks up a small wooden ramp into a covered porch. The camera follows her as she enters the building, revealing a rustic interior with patterned wallpaper, a chandelier made of antlers, and warm lighting.

transcript_segments.text: Riders coming. On a hot day in the Arizona desert, horseback strangers ride into an old west town. They tie up their mounts.

Then spurs jingle as boots clomp on the wooden sidewalk as the strangers head for the saloon. Push through the swinging doors. How about a cold round, you guys? Pull up a seat at the big bar and order up a cold one. Looks like a scene out of the 1800s or maybe some Western movie. But this is real life in the 21st century. Just another fun day at Tombstone Monument Ranch. The only bar in the middle of the desert for miles.

This most unique member of the Dude Ranchers Association is laid out to look just like a town out of the Old West. But behind the doors of what appears to be the mining office, Miss Kitty's bordello, or Doc Holliday's dentist chair, are state-of-the-art rooms, decorated in an 1880s flair. We live in the newspaper. Juergen von Wolf and his wife Karen were here from Germany on a springtime visit to the ranch, their seventh in just five years, with plans for two more trips by the end of the year.

transcript_segments.text: It's the people. It's the scenery. It's the surroundings. It's the horses. It's the personnel. A lot of people ask us, isn't it boring coming over to the same place? And I say, no, because we like horseback riding. We like the area. We like to go out. And that's it. We love it. The couple have been to a dozen dude ranches over the years, but this place is like no other.

Coming out here is something for my wife and me to come back home. The horseback riding is what keeps them coming back on trails that explore some 30,000 acres of pristine Arizona desert. Whether it's a gentle walking ride, or my favorite, those fast lopes offered for experienced riders,

The next shot is a point-of-view shot from a moving horse, looking down a main street lined with adobe-style buildings and bare trees.

transcript_segments.text: Most every horseback trek begins with a ride down Main Street, past the authentic-looking buildings and the family-friendly Trapman Saloon. The Old West ambiance is really cool. The ranch is built on the site of the historic Trapman Ranch, which once included the stagecoach stop where history tells us Doc Holliday boarded the stage when he left Tombstone for good. So anyway, there's a lot of history right here around this ranch. When you're walking around, you'll feel it, and it's just like walking the streets of Tombstone.

That famous town is just two miles away. We'll take you for a visit in just a bit. But even closer stands the namesake of Tombstone Monument Ranch, the 25-foot tall stone monument to Tombstone founder Ed Sheflin. The Apaches ruled this country when the prospector came here looking for gold. Friends warned all he'd find in the desert would be his own tombstone.

transcript_segments.text: And he came close one day when he hid out from an Apache war party in some rocks where that monument now stands, which you can visit on a trail ride. But when Sheflin discovered the silver strike that made him rich, he had the last laugh, naming his new town Tombstone, which would prove to be a fitting name. Here lies George Johnson, hanged by mistake, 1882. He was right, we was wrong, but we strung him up, and now he's gone.

transcript_spans.text: Riders coming. On a hot day in the Arizona desert, horseback strangers ride into an old west town. They tie up their mounts.

Then spurs jingle as boots clomp on the wooden sidewalk as the strangers head for the saloon. Push through the swinging doors. How about a cold round, you guys? Pull up a seat at the big bar and order up a cold one. Looks like a scene out of the 1800s or maybe some Western movie. But this is real life in the 21st century. Just another fun day at Tombstone Monument Ranch. The only bar in the middle of the desert for miles.

This most unique member of the Dude Ranchers Association is laid out to look just like a town out of the Old West. But behind the doors of what appears to be the mining office, Miss Kitty's bordello, or Doc Holliday's dentist chair, are state-of-the-art rooms, decorated in an 1880s flair. We live in the newspaper. Juergen von Wolf and his wife Karen were here from Germany on a springtime visit to the ranch, their seventh in just five years, with plans for two more trips by the end of the year.

transcript_spans.text: It's the people. It's the scenery. It's the surroundings. It's the horses. It's the personnel. A lot of people ask us, isn't it boring coming over to the same place? And I say, no, because we like horseback riding. We like the area. We like to go out. And that's it. We love it. The couple have been to a dozen dude ranches over the years, but this place is like no other.

Coming out here is something for my wife and me to come back home. The horseback riding is what keeps them coming back on trails that explore some 30,000 acres of pristine Arizona desert. Whether it's a gentle walking ride, or my favorite, those fast lopes offered for experienced riders,

transcript_spans.text: Most every horseback trek begins with a ride down Main Street, past the authentic-looking buildings and the family-friendly Trapman Saloon. The Old West ambiance is really cool. The ranch is built on the site of the historic Trapman Ranch, which once included the stagecoach stop where history tells us Doc Holliday boarded the stage when he left Tombstone for good. So anyway, there's a lot of history right here around this ranch. When you're walking around, you'll feel it, and it's just like walking the streets of Tombstone.

That famous town is just two miles away. We'll take you for a visit in just a bit. But even closer stands the namesake of Tombstone Monument Ranch, the 25-foot tall stone monument to Tombstone founder Ed Sheflin. The Apaches ruled this country when the prospector came here looking for gold. Friends warned all he'd find in the desert would be his own tombstone.

transcript_spans.text: And he came close one day when he hid out from an Apache war party in some rocks where that monument now stands, which you can visit on a trail ride.
